topic: intrinsic minimax modulus (functional modulus of continuity) for finite-dimensional functionals of no-solution NPIV least-squares projections, indexed by spectral-decay, source, and functional-representer source classes - the missing converse to the Tikhonov achievability rate of Shen-Kallus et al (arXiv:2604.24660); deliver a sharp intrinsic-modulus characterization with matched lower and upper bounds where available, and claim Tikhonov rate-suboptimality only if an estimator attaining the faster modulus rate is exhibited

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize the intrinsic minimax modulus for finite-dimensional linear functionals of the minimum-norm no-solution NPIV least-squares projection under joint spectral-decay, projected-source, and functional-representer source classes.

source_refs.claim: The paper targets least-squares NPIV projection functionals that remain defined when the primal or dual inverse equation has no solution and gives Tikhonov upper-rate guarantees under projected source conditions.
source_refs.author: Zikai Shen, Nathan Kallus, Dimitri Meunier, Houssam Zenati, Arthur Gretton, Aurelien Bibaut

source_refs.claim: They study minimax-optimal and adaptive estimation of linear functionals in nonparametric instrumental regression, but in the classical NPIR setting rather than no-solution projection functionals.
source_refs.author: Christoph Breunig, Jan Johannes

source_refs.claim: They establish minimax lower and upper rates for NPIV/NPIR function estimation under ill-posedness, leaving finite-dimensional no-solution projection functionals outside the target class.
source_refs.author: Xiaohong Chen, Markus Reiss

source_refs.claim: They show that moduli of continuity govern sharp lower bounds and confidence-interval lengths for linear functionals over convex classes.
source_refs.author: T. Tony Cai, Mark G. Low

source_refs.excerpt: the three independent terms of C_n(P) are both sufficient and necessary
local Riesz discussion leaves a sharper regime classifier open
derive the empirical-process upper scale and an actual matching lower-bound construction

open_problems.why_unsolved: Shen-Kallus et al. supply an achievability theory for a no-solution NPIV projection target, while classical NPIV minimax papers supply lower-bound machinery for different targets and classical exact-solution or whole-function losses. The prior CausalSmith frontier runs flag the same missing object: a necessary-and-sufficient local Riesz or critical-radius classifier with a real lower-bound construction, not a sufficient regularization condition. The unresolved obstacle is to combine the no-solution projection geometry with a spectral least-favorable perturbation argument that simultaneously respects the source class of the projected structural component and the source class of the functional representer.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A theorem giving equivalent upper and lower intrinsic moduli for the target functional over explicit polynomial or exponential singular-value decay classes and source/representer ellipsoids, with constants or sharp rates and a matching estimator where the upper bound is claimed.

writing_exemplar.why: It supplies the motivating no-solution NPIV estimand and the Tikhonov achievability story that the modulus result would complete.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: NPIV inverse equations may have no exact solution -> least-squares projection functionals remain well-defined -> Tikhonov gives upper rates under projected source conditions -> what is the intrinsic minimax modulus for those functionals?

method_exemplars.technique: Use convex-class moduli of continuity to convert least-favorable functional separation under small statistical distance into lower bounds and honest interval-length benchmarks.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The desired object is exactly a modulus for a finite-dimensional linear functional; the transfer is to replace the direct Gaussian model by the NPIV singular-coordinate experiment.

method_exemplars.technique: Analyze spectral estimators for NPIR linear functionals with tuning rules tied to smoothness, ill-posedness, and representer regularity.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is the closest NPIV linear-functional estimation template; the missing step is replacing exact-solution smoothness with projected no-solution source and representer classes.

method_exemplars.technique: Reduce NPIV ill-posedness to singular-value tail calculations and match minimax lower bounds with sieve minimum-distance upper rates.
method_exemplars.why_closest: Its NPIV lower-bound architecture is structurally analogous to the required converse, but the loss must be changed from whole-function risk to a source-weighted projection functional.

open_problems.open_problem: Determine in which no-solution NPIV source and representer regimes first-order Tikhonov is rate-suboptimal, making any suboptimality claim only after exhibiting an estimator attaining the faster intrinsic-modulus rate.

source_refs.claim: Their no-solution NPIV rates are Tikhonov achievability rates and their discussion points to regularization saturation as a possible ceiling rather than an intrinsic lower bound.
source_refs.author: Zikai Shen, Nathan Kallus, Dimitri Meunier, Houssam Zenati, Arthur Gretton, Aurelien Bibaut

source_refs.claim: They use source-condition double-robust inference and iterated/adversarial inverse-problem estimators, showing that estimator qualification matters for functional inference.
source_refs.author: Andrew Bennett, Nathan Kallus, Xiaojie Mao, Whitney Newey, Vasilis Syrgkanis, Masatoshi Uehara

source_refs.claim: They survey spectral regularization, source conditions, and inverse-problem regularization tools used in econometric ill-posed inverse problems.
source_refs.author: Marine Carrasco, Jean-Pierre Florens, Eric Renault

source_refs.claim: They place Tikhonov inside a broader spectral-algorithm family whose qualification determines how source smoothness is converted into convergence rates.
source_refs.author: L. Lo Gerfo, Lorenzo Rosasco, Francesca Odone, Ernesto De Vito, Alessandro Verri

source_refs.excerpt: This is not a novelty claim. It is the finite exact-fiber version of the Tikhonov
withdraws the rejected angle's stronger claim that a scalar smallest singular value alone creates sharp
Give an explicit observable LP/QP recipe for tau_native

open_problems.why_unsolved: The web literature separates regularization qualification from intrinsic statistical difficulty, and the CausalSmith proximal-phase record warns that Tikhonov convergence or a scalar singular-value phase claim is support material rather than a flagship theorem. The concrete obstacle is that a faster modulus does not by itself prove first-order Tikhonov suboptimality for the no-solution target; one must show that residual-debiasing and projected-source geometry still permit a feasible higher-qualification or spectral-cutoff estimator to attain the faster rate.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A regime theorem comparing first-order Tikhonov to an explicit higher-qualification estimator, such as iterated Tikhonov, spectral cutoff, or a modulus-calibrated debiased series estimator, with a proved upper bound matching the intrinsic modulus and a corollary listing exactly the source/representer regimes where first-order Tikhonov is slower.

writing_exemplar.why: It is the paper whose Tikhonov rate should be treated as the baseline, and its no-solution setup prevents importing a classical saturation claim unchanged.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: No-solution NPIV projection targets can be estimated by Tikhonov -> first-order Tikhonov has finite qualification -> higher source smoothness may call for faster spectral methods -> prove faster estimation before calling Tikhonov suboptimal.

method_exemplars.technique: Use source-condition functional inference with adversarial or iterated inverse-problem estimators and track primal and dual approximation errors separately.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The open problem needs a feasible estimator and inference proof, not only a lower modulus; this paper is closest to a higher-qualification causal inverse-problem estimator.

method_exemplars.technique: Compare filter functions and qualification across Tikhonov, iterated Tikhonov, and related spectral algorithms to translate source smoothness into bias rates.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The suspected gap is exactly a qualification gap; the transfer is to insert the no-solution projection functional and variance calculation into the spectral-filter proof.

method_exemplars.technique: Use modulus lower bounds to decide when a proposed faster estimator is actually rate-optimal rather than merely faster than one regularization scheme.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It supplies the guardrail required by the prompt: suboptimality should be relative to the intrinsic modulus, not just relative to Tikhonov.

open_problems.open_problem: Develop adaptive and honest inference for no-solution NPIV projection functionals when the spectral decay, projected-source smoothness, and functional-representer smoothness are unknown.

source_refs.claim: They construct data-driven estimators for NPIR linear functionals that attain optimal rates up to logarithmic factors, after observing that oracle tuning depends on unknown smoothness and operator features.
source_refs.author: Christoph Breunig, Jan Johannes

source_refs.claim: They show Lepski-type adaptive estimation for quadratic NPIV functionals, with exact adaptation in some regimes and logarithmic losses in irregular mildly ill-posed regimes.
source_refs.author: Christoph Breunig, Xiaohong Chen

source_refs.claim: They characterize adaptation for linear functionals over collections of spaces through between-class moduli.
source_refs.author: T. Tony Cai, Mark G. Low

source_refs.claim: They develop inference for semi/nonparametric conditional moment models that can cover regular and irregular functionals, but not no-solution projection-functional moduli.
source_refs.author: Xiaohong Chen, Demian Pouzo

source_refs.claim: They derive optimal sup-norm rates and uniform inference for NPIV functionals under mild and severe ill-posedness, without treating no-solution least-squares projection targets.
source_refs.author: Xiaohong Chen, Timothy M. Christensen

open_problems.why_unsolved: Adaptive NPIV functional work prices unknown smoothness and ill-posedness for classical NPIR targets, and conditional-moment inference papers handle regular and irregular functionals under their own identification structure. The no-solution target adds projected residual directions and separate primal and representer source classes, so the relevant adaptive lower bound is a between-class modulus for projection functionals, not the oracle tuning problem already solved for exact-solution NPIV.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An adaptive confidence-interval or estimator theorem whose risk or interval length matches the no-solution between-class modulus over nested spectral/source/representer classes, together with impossibility or logarithmic-loss statements for regimes where honest adaptation is blocked.

writing_exemplar.why: It frames the same practical problem of NPIV functional tuning under unknown smoothness and operator decay, which can be redirected to the no-solution projection target.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: NPIV functional rates depend on unknown smoothness and ill-posedness -> oracle regularization is infeasible -> adaptive procedures need a price for ignorance -> what is the honest adaptive price for no-solution projection functionals?

method_exemplars.technique: Use between-class moduli to characterize when adaptation over nested classes is possible and how much risk or interval length must inflate.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The desired result is an adaptation theorem indexed by multiple source classes; between-class moduli are the natural object.

method_exemplars.technique: Apply Lepski selection over NPIV regularization levels and prove regime-dependent exact or logarithmically degraded adaptation.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It supplies an NPIV-specific adaptive proof pattern, including irregular-regime log losses, that can be ported to linear no-solution projection functionals.

method_exemplars.technique: Use sieve conditional-moment inference for functionals that may be irregular, with variance and approximation terms controlled in a unified statistic.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The no-solution projection target still sits in a conditional-moment/inverse-problem experiment, so this gives the closest broad inference architecture for moving from rates to confidence sets.

literature_map:
Shen, Kallus, Meunier, Zenati, Gretton, and Bibaut (2026, arXiv:2604.24660) introduce no-solution NPIV least-squares projection targets and Tikhonov upper-rate inference under projected source conditions.
Their rate statements make the missing converse precise: is the Tikhonov rate the intrinsic minimax modulus or only an achievability bound for one spectral filter?
Breunig and Johannes (2011, arXiv:1109.0961) is the closest classical NPIR linear-functional minimax/adaptation paper, but it assumes the classical target rather than the no-solution projection functional.
Chen and Reiss (2011, Econometric Theory, arXiv:0709.2003) and Hall and Horowitz (2006, Annals of Statistics) give NPIV minimax rate templates for whole-function losses under mild/severe ill-posedness.
Bennett, Kallus, Mao, Newey, Syrgkanis, and Uehara (2023, arXiv:2307.13793) develop source-condition double-robust inference for inverse-problem functionals and show why estimator qualification matters.
Carrasco, Florens, and Renault (2007, Handbook of Econometrics) provide the econometric spectral-regularization background for source conditions and ill-posedness.
Lo Gerfo, Rosasco, Odone, De Vito, and Verri (2008, Neural Computation) make the regularization-qualification issue explicit across spectral algorithms.
Cai and Low (2005, arXiv:math/0503662; 2006, arXiv:math/0602299) provide the modulus and between-class modulus tools for sharp functional lower bounds and adaptation.
Armstrong and Kolesar (2015/2016, arXiv:1511.06028) show how modulus arguments become optimal inference for linear functionals over convex classes.
Breunig and Chen (2021, arXiv:2101.12282) show adaptive NPIV functional estimation can be exact in some regimes and pay logarithmic losses in irregular regimes.
Chen and Pouzo (2014, arXiv:1411.1144) and Chen and Christensen (2015, arXiv:1508.03365) provide conditional-moment and NPIV inference templates for irregular functionals, but not no-solution projection moduli.
The live gap is the fusion: no-solution NPIV has a well-defined projection estimand and upper rates, while minimax modulus theory has the right lower-bound language, but the intrinsic modulus, estimator-matching regimes, and adaptive inference theory have not been written down together.
prior_proposal_map:
Mined direct target path stat_npiv_nosol_modulus: only state and pipeline initialization exist, so there is no prior proposal or review for this qid yet.
Most relevant prior signal comes from eid_dml_orthogonality_frontier_v1: its proposal and review critiques say the advertised frontier needed a sharper local Riesz classifier and an actual matching lower-bound construction.
Stat_ate_overlap_decay contributes the caution that minimax frontier claims require shell-sensitive lower bounds rather than upper-frontier heuristics.
Eid_proximal_phase_v1 supplies the closest Tikhonov/ill-posed analogy: Tikhonov convergence was treated as support material, and a scalar smallest-singular-value phase claim was withdrawn.
The eid_proximal_phase reviewer strengthening paths also ask for value-level observable recipes rather than endpoint bookkeeping, which supports posing the NPIV target as a functional-modulus problem.
Q1_spectral_phase_transition contributes a spectral caution: row-space algebra and statistical information loss must be separated, especially around boundary singular directions.
No Track-A source alone survived as a final standalone entry; the high-quality prior signals were used to cross-link and discipline the two web-anchored NPIV gaps.
